replies.author: @BillSPrestonEsq.69
replies.text:
@cannabishornliu6119 "The world is ending and Will thinks it's the appropriate time to confirm he's gay?" The problem is How they did it, not that they did it. I like the idea that Vecna gets in your head and exploits your insecurities and such -- so Will being honest with everyone denies Vecna that power. That's a good story point! If we change "I don't like girls" to "I did X thing that hurt Y character", then I think folks would see it's a good story point. The problem is that everyone has to stop saving the world for this drawn out confession which turns into (unrealistic) affirmation. Imagine if Will had opened up about this in the truck on their drive, and instead of a group hug his brother puts a hand on his shoulder and says, "Will, you're always my brother" and his friends (from their seats) nod in agreement or something.

The scene they wrote is bad, but parts of the framework are OK in principle - it's the execution that's the issue.

replies.author: @ChewsCarefully
replies.text:
That's _part_ of it. As someone who loved the outdoors & hated sitting watching other people live their lives movies never held any appeal for me. I think that a huge part of 'going to the movies' was sitting in air conditioned rooms & forgetting about the world for 90 minutes.
But this is where Hollywood did it to themselves: watch movies from the early '80s. Because at some point before the '90s everything changed. Most people wouldn't include "Lethal Weapon" as part of anything bad but it was because Nothing Surprising Happened in that movie. They copied & pasted the "villain resurrection" & every not-real plot twist along the way & if you get over making googly-eyes at Mel Gibson, it's as clunky as an M. Knight Shyamalan movie. (I just remembered after typing this that Mel was in one of them. Ah well). 
The other factor is it's all your own fault. Elevating Star Wars to anything but the 'C' movie it ever was. I love the description of Darth & Obi Wan's battle as looking like two seniors waking up & fighting with their canes. The entire story could have been summed up by "& then a deus ex mulligan saved the day & honestly, made it feel as if none of the characters mattered." I saw that movie first when I was _nine_ & felt that since the movie itself dismissed itself, all its characters & made all the stakes irrelevant via magic, I wasn't going to concern myself over any part of it. & I *_LOVE_* sci-fi!!
It was as if you all *wanted* the crappiest of films & franchises over quality & fresh ideas. How do none of you realize how empty, score-dependent & poster-central SW was? There was no story that mattered once Luke used The Force.
Name me any other movie that would be improved by a voice-over & boring effect to end it like a child wishing all the troubles away. Isn't that what you all whine about with Mary Sues with no flaws now? People try to argue that Luke Skywalker trained etc. Take away The Force: what would any of his training have accomplished against the Death Star? That's exactly the point, The Force diminishes everything. 
Luke could have stayed at home & wished for a Direct Hit from afar. The whole movie voided itself. & you all wanted more. I honestly don't get it. How do you praise movies that pull happy endings out of their arses yet expect better of Hollywood? You voted with your $ & now everything _is_ Star Wars & plot, characterization & merchandising opportunities is the norm. It's like you got exactly what you all wanted & now that's someone else's fault. But SW _is_ a paint by numbers movie where you see the whole page & the pattern on it from the moment the curtains rise.

Like a lot of people, I had no interest in KPop Demon Hunters when I saw the trailer 

I just thought the animation looked great but the story seemed kinda corny and not a fan of KPop. Then someone uploaded all of “How’s It’s Done” on Twitter and that got me hooked and went “Alright movie, I’ll give you a shot then”; the rest is golden history

Frankly I was reminded of movies from the Disney Renaissance era with how likable Rumi, Mira and Zoey are without sacrificing their personalities and charms, a story with a huge love for Korean mythology and a universal message of struggle and acceptance with the classic good vs evil in there, excellent animation, absolutely banger songs and so much more! My only complaints were that this should have been released in theaters instead of just on Netflix and now I want more about the girls, the lore behind the hunters and so much more!!

And the fact that even people who are not even the target demographic for the movie absolutely adore this movie as well tells you this was movie we have all been waiting for especially since it took them almost a decade to make this masterpiece!

replies.author: @victorcates9330
replies.text:
Recently, I watched 'For A Few Dollars', 'For a Few Dollars More' and 'The Good, the bad, and the ugly'. They have b movie trappings (mistakes, dubbing). But they're so much more vivid than anything modern I've seen this year. I considered writing a negative review comparing them to Thunderbolts and expressing my outrage that none of these movies featured 4 characters spending ten minutes climbing up an elevator shaft. Movies have become plastic. You watch old movies with people who haven't had teeth whitening and those faces were shaped by doing other things with their lives and then you watch a modern movie. It's like moving from watching a human to watching a mannequin. The CGI is also a killer. You watch old films with shattered metal. It feels real. You watch a CGI film with zero natural lighting, limited airflow, and dust and your brain knows there's something wrong.  Modern films feel like photoshopped images. You don't immediately register the problem, but everything is subtly off in a hundred different ways.

You look at Jaws and it's often strikingly beautiful. But you're also aware that the two leads aren't pretty enough that they'd get regular work in current Hollywood. The human leads, I meant. But Bruce could work out more.

replies.text:
They are, well to you. Each year that passes, becomes a smaller fraction of your total life, so then you perceive a year in a more compressed timeline. When your 4 years old, a year has been 1/4 of your whole life. When you're 25 it's only 1/25. 60, 1/60 and so on.

 It sucks that we feel time in this manner, imagine if a year felt the same at 25 as it did at age 4. This is also why what happens when we're younger sticks with us so much more than when we're older. That week you cried over a break up, felt so much longer at 16 than it does at 30.

author: @thekitsguy
text: Drinker, you are hands down the best movie critic on YouTube. I love film and worked in the industry for over 20 years, and I’ve often thought about starting a review channel myself. Then I stop and ask how I could possibly top what you’re already doing and I honestly don’t think I could. I’m really looking forward to what the channel brings next, and I wish you continued success in 2026.

replies.author: @Ralathar44
replies.text:
I'd have agreed with you at one point but I think he's been slowly losing his way.  Pretty much first noticed it with the Great Fallout debate.  Felt like a small part of Drinker died that die, buried under the permanent filibuster of Mauler, and he's leaned a few degrees more negative since then and I think it's slowly getting worse.  He'd prolly already be negative about some of the things he'd previously liked or recommended before.  And regardless of how good or bad or mid Superman was he and the crew hated that movie like a year before it ever even came to theaters.  Using their own metric (2.5x production budget) Superman has been a great success, with 615 million revenue vs a 225 million budget (2.5x that is 562 million) leaving a profit of 80 million.

I still follow and watch, but if the trend continues I'll prolly eventually unsubscribe.

author: @Ace_Shadowrun
text:
Cinema experience was going to disappear, but COVID sped up the fall by 10 years, and no surprise. We are living through a global recession, if you have a family of 4, going to the movies costs you around $100, plus the time to get there and to watch only one film, for a fraction of that cost, you pay a streaming service for a month, yes, it's not the same experience, but the time and money savings are very big. 10 years ago, I wouldn't have believed the era of movie theatres would end, but here we are now.

Great video, I thought War of the World would make it, but nope, it was a very contested year.

author: @EdmondGuay
text: Thank you so much for riding high and fast into my YouTube feed. Your content is truly one-of-a-kind writing with enough metaphors, similes, and allusions to fill a freshmen college English class.  Pure Delight.  My gratitude.  Thanks for this closing retrospect on all things icky and good in 2025…only one thing makes me truly cry — your town crier warning of the demise of movie houses.  That idea makes me sad beyond words.  I grew up sneaking out of my house as a pre-teen to check out the movies at the local theatres, became obsessed with the movie world, scored a job as a doorman at a movie houses that premiered both ROCKY and NETWORK in the same week. Then eventually graduated to a manager and projectionist of a second run movie house that  was a neighborhood favorite and one that locked in midnight festivals of Woody Allen, Star Wars, Rocky Horror, Harold and Maude, and tons of others.  I love going to the movies and sinking my teeth into the popcorn with golden flavored topping, sneaking in a sandwich and drink and escaping into films on the big screen with big sound systems.  Because no matter what I do at home, nothing can replicate that.  The 1950s saw a similar demise on the horizon with the advent of television, but 25 years later STAR WARS rescued the movie house and invented the cineplex of 15+ screens in one locale.  Maybe that can happen again…but at this rate the ticket price will be equivalent to a second mortgage on my house.  Anyway…that’s all I got.

 Unlike with American cartoons in Japan cartoons are not targeted at children specifically and instead of developing from kids comics like in the West they developed from things like kabuki theater street side plays. replies.author: @BurtonShotton
replies.text:
@milivoi10 
I actually consider anime/manga and other forms of "graphic art" or "cartoon art" to be superior to much of what hangs in modern art museums.
I maintain that graphic artists such as Boris Vallejo and Frank Frazetta are/were some of the best artists of the 20th century.
I have a rule of thumb for art - I'm no artist; if I can do it, it ain't art.  
Sorry to the abstract expressionists, but I've seen stuff painted by zoo elephants that is nearly indistinguishable from "modern art".
